message:
fix(storage,sync): magnitude-aware epoch migration + combined-error handling for import FK restore

Two related fixes to the recently-landed v6 datetime migration and the
JSONL-import foreign-key restoration path.

src/storage/schema.rs — `repair_integer_datetime_columns`:
- The original v6 migration hard-coded `{column} / 1000000.0` on the
  assumption that all integer-typed datetime values were microseconds.
  But the runtime reader (`datetime_from_epoch_auto` in
  `src/storage/sqlite.rs`) explicitly handles seconds / ms / µs / ns
  by magnitude, and the integer values observed in the wild come from
  more than one source (chrono `.timestamp_millis()` and some
  third-party tools that write seconds). Uniform `/1000000` silently
  mis-converts three of the four unit classes — a row written as ms
  would land in year 58,420 AD after the migration.
- Replace the hard-coded divisor with a CASE expression that matches
  the reader's magnitude bucketing exactly:
    ABS < 10^10           → seconds  (`* 1.0`)
    ABS < 10^13           → ms       (`/ 1000.0`)
    ABS < 10^16           → µs       (`/ 1000000.0`)
    otherwise             → ns       (`/ 1000000000.0`)
  Each threshold is the smallest integer value that, in its unit, still
  lands within ±2286 AD, so the buckets are non-overlapping for any
  realistic beads timestamp. Comment explicitly ties this to
  `datetime_from_epoch_auto` and warns future editors that the two must
  stay in lock-step.
- Cleaner output shape: `strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%fZ', ...)` now
  produces the trailing Z in a single call instead of the previous
  `strftime(...) || 'Z'` concatenation.
- Updated docstring: "The unit (seconds / ms / µs / ns) is inferred
  from magnitude exactly like the runtime reader's
  `datetime_from_epoch_auto`."

src/storage/schema.rs — test:
- Expand `test_rebuild_repairs_integer_datetime_columns` from a single
  "bug-1" row (microseconds only) to four rows named
  `bug-sec/bug-ms/bug-us/bug-ns`, each encoding the same instant
  `2026-04-20T02:18:08Z` in its respective unit. Post-migration each
  row must decode back to that instant — the previous test would have
  silently passed while three of the four rows were corrupted.

src/sync/mod.rs — `import_from_jsonl`:
- Extract `finish_import_after_foreign_key_restore(apply_result,
  fk_restore_result)` from the match-on-two-Results chain at the end
  of the import flow. The four-arm match was duplicated between the
  happy path and the error path only to call
  `progress.finish_with_message` vs `progress.finish_and_clear`;
  pulling the Result-collapsing into a pure helper lets the outer
  function choose the progress-finish shape based on the final
  outcome and gives us a testable pure function.
- When BOTH apply and FK-restore failed, return
  `BeadsError::WithContext` that names the FK-restore failure in
  the context string AND wraps the original import error as the
  source. Previously the FK-restore error was logged at error level
  and silently discarded — the caller received only the import
  error, so a concurrent "import failed AND we couldn't put FKs
  back on" incident was invisible to the CLI JSON surface.
- New unit test
  `test_import_error_reports_foreign_key_restore_failure_when_both_fail`
  pins the combined-error shape so future refactors can't regress it.

tests/conformance.rs, tests/e2e_workspace_scenarios.rs:
- Small touch-ups on the just-landed helper extractions: label
  strings for `assert_command_success` tightened to spell the
  command name on one conformance assertion, and a doctor-check
  assertion switched from a direct key-index to the new
  `assert_doctor_json_has_healthy_checks` helper in one scenario.
  No behaviour change.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
